elements.sent-2411-001: Music captioning has emerged as a promising task, fueled by the advent of advanced language generation models.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the evaluation of music captioning relies heavily on traditional metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE which were developed for other domains, without proper justification for their use in this new field.
elements.sent-2411-003: We present cases where traditional metrics are vulnerable to syntactic changes, and show they do not correlate well with human judgments.
elements.sent-2411-004: By addressing these issues, we aim to emphasize the need for a critical reevaluation of how music captions are assessed.
elements.sent-2411-005: The advancement of music information retrieval (MIR) parallels significant developments in the music industry, particularly regarding tasks like music captioning \cite{muscaps, lpmusiccaps, gardner2023llark, li2023mert, enriching .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Despite the promise of music captioning, the evaluation of generated captions poses significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-007: Current metrics, borrowed from natural language processing tasks, do not adequately address the unique qualities of musical content.
elements.sent-2411-008: These traditional metrics, such as BLEU\cite{papineni2002bleu , METEOR\cite{meteor , and ROUGE\cite{lin2004rouge , primarily rely on n-gram overlap, focusing on superficial similarities between generated and reference captions \cite{gardner2023llark .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This approach inherently favors syntactic similarity over semantic meaning, which may result in misleading evaluations of caption quality.
elements.sent-2411-010: For example, a paraphrased caption may receive a substantially lower score, even though it conveys the same core idea as the original.
elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, these metrics fail to effectively capture the semantics of musical contexts, such as genre, instruments, and mood.
elements.sent-2411-012: This can result in evaluations that may overlook critical aspects of the music being described, leading to poor alignment with how humans perceive and interpret musical content.
elements.sent-2411-013: This work aims to illustrate the limitations of these traditional metrics, and highlight instances where they score inaccurately high.
elements.sent-2411-014: To evaluate the effectiveness of traditional metrics in music captioning, we conduct a human evaluation study aimed at correlating human judgments with the scores generated by these metrics.
elements.sent-2411-015: We use Amazon Mechanical Turk\cite{mturk to recruit 50 participants for a listening test.}
elements.sent-2411-016: From the evaluation set of the MusicCaps \cite{musiclm dataset, we randomly sample 30 audio clips.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Each audio sample is paired with three different types of captions:.
elements.sent-2411-018: Original: The ground truth caption associated with the audio in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-019: Inference: The caption generated by the audio captioning model.
elements.sent-2411-020: For the inference, we use music captioning model by LP-MusicCaps \cite{lpmusiccaps .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Paraphrased: A version of the original caption syntactically altered without changing the meaning.
elements.sent-2411-022: Participants are asked to evaluate how accurately the captions describe the audio clips.
elements.sent-2411-023: To maintain the integrity of the evaluation and minimize potential bias, each participant is presented with only two of the three caption versions for each audio sample.
elements.sent-2411-024: This design helps prevent participants from recognizing that two of three captions are likely ground truth captions, while the other one is not.

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{figs/file3.png} \caption{Experimental results on scores across caption type and evaluation metrics from MusicCaps evaluation set. MOS values are rescaled to [0, 1].} \label{fig:syntax}
elements.sent-2411-025: Following the collection of human evaluation scores, we report the scores of evaluation metrics (including the Mean Opinion Score (MOS)) for original and paraphrased captions, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:syntax .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Notably, evaluation metrics except for FENSE show a significant decrease when comparing the original captions to their paraphrased versions.
elements.sent-2411-027: This indicates that these metrics may be overly sensitive to syntactic changes rather than semantic content.
elements.sent-2411-028: As illustrated in Table~\ref{showcase , we also showcase examples of various caption types along with their corresponding evaluation scores.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We also include distorted captions that are semantically altered while preserving their syntactic structure.
elements.sent-2411-030: Notably, most n-gram-based metrics tend to favor distorted captions over paraphrased captions.
elements.sent-2411-031: To further validate our findings, we compute the correlation coefficient between the scores obtained from human evaluations and each of the evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-032: As presented in Table~\ref{correlation_coefficient , results indicate that most evaluation metrics, including FENSE\cite{fense , do not show significant correlations with human evaluations.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: We demonstrate existing metrics are overly sensitive to syntactic variations, and they lack alignment with actual human evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Given these findings, we conclude that a more nuanced evaluation framework is necessary to address these challenges.
